1: meta pente hēmeras katebē ho archiereus Ananias ('after five days the high priest Ananias went down'): the high priest himself travels from Jerusalem to Caesarea — an unusual step that signals the seriousness of the case to the Jerusalem leadership. Rhētoros Tertullou tinos ('a certain orator named Tertullus'): Tertullus is a professional advocate trained in Latin forensic rhetoric — the first explicit appearance of a professional lawyer in Acts. His name is Roman; he may be a Romanized Jew or a Gentile hired for his rhetorical expertise.
2: pollēs eirēnēs tynchanontes ('we enjoy much peace'): Tertullus's opening is a masterpiece of Roman forensic flattery — the captatio benevolentiae required by rhetorical convention. The claim of peace and prosperity under Felix is historically ironic; Felix's tenure was marked by significant unrest and brutal suppression of various movements (Josephus, Ant. 20.7-8; Wars 2.13). The flattery is formulaic rather than factual, which Tertullus knows and Felix knows; the game of rhetoric proceeds anyway.
3: pantē te kai pantachou apodechometha, kratiste Phēlix, meta pasēs eucharistias ('everywhere and in every way we acknowledge this with profound gratitude, most excellent Felix'): the accumulated superlatives — everywhere, every way, most excellent, profound gratitude — pile up to exaggerate the compliment. The very density of the flattery signals its conventional character. Roman forensic oratory expected such openings; the court expected them; Felix would have measured the speech by its argumentative quality rather than its compliments.
4: kleptō se epi pleion ('I do not want to detain you further'): the brevitas topos — claiming brevity before a long speech — was a standard rhetorical device. Tertullus's actual speech is relatively brief; his promise of brevity is kept more than most orators who invoke it. Tē sē epieikeia ('your indulgence/clemency'): appealing to the judge's character-virtue (epieikeia = fairness, reasonableness) as the ground for a favorable hearing is conventional advocacy.
5: loimos ('a pest/plague'): a stock insult in ancient rhetoric for political troublemakers and social agitators. Prōtostatēs ('ringleader/chief man'): a military term for the man in the front rank, applied here as 'leader' of the sect. Hairesis ('sect'): the first application of this term to the Nazarenes in Acts. Hairesis in Paul's usage (1 Cor 11:19; Gal 5:20) is pejorative — 'faction, divisive party'; Tertullus uses it to characterize Christianity as a schismatic movement within Judaism rather than a legitimate tradition.
6: ton hieron epeiresen bebēlōsai ('he tried to desecrate the temple'): the charge is the most serious — temple desecration was a capital offense under Jewish law (Josephus confirms Gentiles faced execution for entering the inner courts, Wars 6.2.4). The actual charge (21:28) was that Paul brought Trophimus the Ephesian into the temple — not proven, and now the accusers have dropped to the vaguer 'tried to desecrate.' The case is deteriorating legally from an alleged specific act to an attempted offense.
7: The verse (omitted in some manuscripts; included in the textus receptus) accuses Lysias of forcible intervention in Jewish jurisdiction. Its authenticity is uncertain; it was possibly added to explain the transition in v.8 from the temple arrest to the examination of Paul. The Western text's expansion of Acts at this point is a known textual tendency; most modern translations bracket or omit v.7.
8: para hou dynēsē autos anakrinas peri pantōn toutōn epignōnai ('from whom you will be able, by examining him yourself, to find out the truth about all these charges'): Tertullus refers the decisive evidence to Paul himself — an unusual rhetorical strategy. He assumes Felix's examination will confirm the charges. The confidence reflects either genuine assurance in the case or a rhetorical bluff relying on Felix's unfamiliarity with the details.
9: synepethento de kai hoi Ioudaioi phaskontes tauta houtōs echein ('the Jews also joined in the accusation, asserting that these things were so'): the congregation of accusers (synepetithento, literally 'attacked together') is a collective witness endorsement. In Roman law, multiple witnesses were important; the concurrence of the high priest, elders, and the crowd provides what appears to be overwhelming testimony. Paul's response must counter all of it.
10: ek pollōn etōn onta se kritēn tō ethnei toutō epistamenos ('knowing that you have been a judge over this nation for many years'): Paul's captatio benevolentiae is noticeably shorter and more measured than Tertullus's — he compliments Felix's experience rather than his beneficence. Experience as a judge is a neutral compliment; it appeals to Felix's judicial competence rather than his generosity. Paul's shorter opener models the compressed advocacy style he will employ throughout.
11: ou pleiōn eisin moi hēmerai dōdeka ('it has not been more than twelve days'): Paul introduces temporal specificity as a legal argument — the brevity of his Jerusalem visit is incompatible with the charge of organizing widespread agitation. Twelve days is not enough time to build the kind of movement the accusers describe. The chronological argument is one of three positive defenses Paul offers (the others being eyewitness verification, v.12, and the absence of proper witnesses, v.19).
12: oute en tō hierō heuron me pros tina dialegomonon ('they did not find me arguing with anyone at the temple'): Paul's denial is categorical and specific — not arguing in the temple, not stirring up crowds in the synagogues, not creating disturbances anywhere in the city. The tripartite denial directly addresses Tertullus's three-part charge (troublemaker, ringleader of a sect, temple desecrator). The legal form of the defense mirrors the form of the accusation.
13: oude parastēsai dynantai soi peri hōn nyn katēgorousin mou ('nor can they prove to you the charges they are now bringing against me'): the burden-of-proof argument — paristēmi (prove, demonstrate) is a legal term — places the evidentiary burden where Roman law placed it: on the accusers. Paul does not merely deny; he challenges the accusers to produce verifiable evidence. The challenge will go unanswered (v.19 — the real witnesses are not present).
14: kata tēn hodon ēn legousin hairesin ('according to the Way which they call a sect'): Paul's reappropriation of Tertullus's hairesis (v.5) is a rhetorical counter. He accepts the label ('which they call a sect') while denying its pejorative force — the Way is not a destructive faction but the fulfillment of the ancestral faith. Latreuon tō patrōō theō ('worshipping the ancestral God') and pisteuōn pasin tois kata ton nomon kai tois en tois prophētais gegrammēnois ('believing all things in the Law and the Prophets') — Christianity is not apostasy from Israel's Scripture but its fulfillment.
15: elpida echōn eis ton theon ēn kai autoi houtoi prosdechontai ('having a hope toward God which these men themselves also hold'): Paul's counter-framing finds common ground with the Pharisees in the Sanhedrin delegation — the hope of resurrection. Anastasin mellein esesthai dikaiōn te kai adikōn ('that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked'): the universal resurrection is from Dan 12:2, the most explicit OT text for bodily resurrection of both groups. Paul's hope is within Israel's canonical expectation.
16: aproskopon syneidēsin echein pros ton theon kai tous anthrōpous dia pantos ('to have a clear conscience toward God and man always'): aproskopos (not stumbling, causing no offense) + syneidēsis (conscience) — the same vocabulary cluster as 23:1. Paul's consistent use of syneidēsis before both Jewish (23:1) and Roman (24:16) audiences shows the term's cross-cultural currency. The phrase acknowledges a dual accountability — to God and to human community — without subordinating either.
17: dia etōn pleionōn... poiēsōn eis to ethnos mou eleēmosynas kai prosphoras ('after several years... bringing alms to my nation and offerings'): Luke's only reference to Paul's collection from Gentile churches for Jerusalem's poor (cf. 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28; 1 Cor 16:1-3). The collection was Paul's major organizational project of his third missionary journey — evidence of the Gentile churches' solidarity with the Jerusalem community. Paul presents this not as a doctrinal claim but as verifiable action: he came for charity and worship, not agitation.
18: hēgnismenon en tō hierō ('having completed the purification rite in the temple'): Paul's temple presence was in the context of fulfilled purification vows — precisely the most Jewish possible activity, not desecration. He was in the state of ritual purity (hagnismenon), performing the socially approved act of completing a Nazirite-related vow, when the accusation arose. The charge of desecration collapses against the actual circumstances of his temple visit.
19: hous edei epi sou pareinai kai katēgorein ('who ought to be present before you and make an accusation'): the absent-witnesses argument is Paul's strongest legal point. Roman law required accusers to appear in person (cf. the Lysias letter, 23:30; Trajan's rescript to Pliny, Ep. 10.97). The Asian Jews who originally made the charge are not present; the Sanhedrin is representing a case it did not witness. This procedural deficiency should, by Roman law, end the case.
20: e autoi houtoi eipatōsan ti heuron adikēma ('or else let these men here state what crime they found in me'): Paul invites the present accusers — those who were at the Sanhedrin hearing — to state their case directly. The only specific charge they can offer is the theological one: resurrection (v.21). Everything else is allegation without evidence. Paul's advocacy is tight and specific; he is forcing the accusers to narrow to the actual substantive disagreement.
21: peri anastaseōs nekrōn egō krinomai sēmeron eph' hymōn ('it is concerning the resurrection of the dead that I am being judged today before you'): Paul's self-summary of the issue cuts through all the political and procedural noise to the theological core. The resurrection is the real question; everything else is obscuring it. His use of the first person singular (egō krinomai) — 'I am being judged' — owns the situation without apology. The statement is both legally accurate and theologically definitive.
22: akribEsteron eidōs ta peri tēs hodou ('knowing the Way more accurately'): Felix's knowledge of the Way — whether through his Jewish wife Drusilla, through administrative experience in Judea, or through other sources — is historically plausible. Anebaleato autous ('he adjourned the proceedings'): the verb signals a formal procedural postponement, not dismissal. Felix neither convicts nor acquits; his deferral is a judicial non-decision driven by political calculation.
23: echein te anesin ('to have some freedom/relief'): Paul's custody is custodia libera (free custody) — not imprisonment in a cell but house arrest with supervision. Kai mēdena kōlyein tōn idiōn autou hypēretein autō ('no one to prevent his own people from serving him'): the apostolic community can access Paul, bring resources, and maintain relationship. This explains how Paul maintained his ministry connections during the Caesarean imprisonment and presumably wrote or dictated letters during this period (possibly including Philippians or Philemon).
24: Drusilla, gynaiki ousē Ioudaia ('with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish'): Drusilla was the youngest daughter of Herod Agrippa I (who died in Acts 12:23) and the sister of Agrippa II (who will appear in chs. 25-26). She had left her previous husband Azizus of Emesa to marry Felix — a marriage that violated both Jewish law (marriage to an uncircumcised Gentile) and social convention. Her Jewish identity made her particularly well-placed to understand Paul's arguments. Pisteos tēs eis Christon Iēsoun ('faith in Christ Jesus'): Paul speaks directly to the theological core even before the most powerful audience he has had.
25: peri dikaiosynēs kai enkrateias kai tou krimatos tou mellontos ('about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come'): the three themes are an apostolic précis of the ethical and eschatological claims of the gospel. Dikaiosynē (righteousness) before a corrupt governor; enkrateia (self-control) to a man who had induced another man's wife to leave her husband; krima mellon (the coming judgment) to a man who had misused his judicial power. Paul's message is precisely calibrated to Felix's moral situation. Emphobos genomenos ('becoming afraid') — Felix's fear is the recognition of personal moral exposure.
26: chrēmata dothēsetai autō hypo tou Paulou ('money would be given to him by Paul'): Felix's hope for a bribe — chrēmata (money) — shows the corruption that Luke has been implying. The frequency of Felix's conversations with Paul (pyknōteron metepempto auton kai hōmilei autō, 'he sent for him frequently and conversed with him') reveals mixed motives: some genuine interest (perhaps through Drusilla) and mercenary calculation. Paul's readiness to speak to Felix repeatedly despite the bribe-hope is his sustained witness to a powerful, sinful audience.
27: dietias de plērōtheis ... katēleipen ton Paulon dedemenon ('after two years had passed... he left Paul imprisoned'): Felix's replacement by Porcius Festus (c. AD 59) ends the Caesarean episode. Felix's final act — leaving Paul imprisoned charin katathesthai tois Ioudaiois ('to show favor to the Jews') — is a straightforwardly political decision: sacrifice a prisoner's legal rights to maintain political relationships. The two-year imprisonment is the time between Paul's arrival in Caesarea (24:27) and his eventual appeal to Caesar, the event that will send him to Rome and fulfill the divine mandate of 23:11.
